bibliographic_data.title: Wake up and join me! An energy-efficient algorithm for maximal matching in radio networks
bibliographic_data.abstract: We consider networks of small, autonomous devices that communicate with each other wirelessly. Minimizing energy usage is an important consideration in designing algorithms for such networks, as battery life is a crucial and limited resource. Working in a model where both sending and listening for messages deplete energy, we consider the problem of finding a maximal matching of the nodes in a radio network of arbitrary and unknown topology. We present a distributed randomized algorithm that produces, with high probability, a maximal matching. The maximum energy cost per node is O(log2 n), and the time complexity is O(∆log n). Here n is any upper bound on the number of nodes, and ∆is any upper bound on the maximum degree; n and ∆are parameters of our algorithm that we assume are known a priori to all the processors. We also consider the related problem of assigning, for each node in the network, a neighbor to back up its data in case of eventual node failure. Here, a key goal is to minimize the maximum load, defined as the number of nodes assigned to a single node. We present an efficient decentralized low-energy algorithm that finds a neighbor assignment whose maximum load is at most a polylog(n) factor bigger than the optimum.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents a distributed randomized algorithm for finding a maximal matching in radio networks with energy cost O(log2 n) and time complexity O(∆log n).
core_content.key_methodology: A distributed randomized algorithm is used to find a maximal matching. The algorithm ensures that the maximum energy cost per node is O(log2 n), and the time complexity is O(∆log n).
outcomes: The algorithm produces, with high probability, a maximal matching.
The maximum load for neighbor assignment is at most a polylog(n) factor bigger than the optimum.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This paper addresses the problem of finding large sets of pairwise disjoint edges (matchings) in radio networks. The authors present an energy-efficient algorithm that finds a maximal matching with a maximum energy cost per node of O(log2 n) and time complexity of O(∆log n). Additionally, they propose a decentralized low-energy algorithm for assigning neighbors to back up data in case of node failures, ensuring the maximum load is minimized.
